text:
GENITOURINARY MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review / Treatment Planning Visit

ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
CC: [Certified Coordinator]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT:
Mr. Hutchinson is a 72-year-old male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) who presents for oncology follow-up and treatment planning following discontinuation of olaparib due to disease progression with visceral metastases and cumulative treatment-related anemia. His case was reviewed at the GU Oncology Multidisciplinary Tumor Board earlier this week. He is accompanied today by his wife.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Hutchinson has a well-documented history of high-risk prostate adenocarcinoma (Gleason 9, cT3bN1M0) diagnosed approximately five years ago, initially treated with ADT plus abiraterone (Zytiga) and definitive IMRT to the prostate and pelvis. He enjoyed a prolonged biochemical response but developed castration-resistant disease approximately two years into that regimen with a PSA doubling time around 3.5 months. Germline testing at that time confirmed a pathogenic BRCA2 frameshift mutation (c.5946delT), and Foundation One CDx on re-biopsy demonstrated biallelic BRCA2 loss, AR amplification, TP53 R175H, RB1 loss, and PTEN deletion. PSMA PET/CT at CRPC declaration showed low-volume disease: a right iliac chain node and a single early bone lesion at the right posterior iliac crest not seen on conventional bone scan.

He was transitioned to enzalutamide (Xtandi) 160 mg daily given the AR amplification pattern on NGS and initial PSA response (61% decline). He achieved a PSA nadir of 0.7 ng/mL with stable imaging at 6 months. However, at 9 months he developed rapid biochemical and radiographic progression -- PSA doubling time of approximately 6 weeks -- with new bone metastases at T10, right 6th rib, and left iliac wing on bone scan and CT. MRI spine confirmed T10 vertebral body involvement without epidural extension or cord compromise. He received palliative RT to T10 (30 Gy in 10 fractions) concurrently with transitioning to olaparib (Lynparza) 300 mg BID based on his biallelic BRCA2 loss.

On olaparib, he had an excellent initial PSA response (nadir 0.6 ng/mL at 6 months) with stable bone disease on restaging scans. However, restaging imaging at 6 months revealed a new 1.4 cm segment VII hepatic lesion, which was biopsied and confirmed as metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma without neuroendocrine transformation (PSMA-positive, NKX3.1-positive, Ki-67 35%). Olaparib was continued given bone and PSA stability, but over the subsequent 2 months PSA rose to 1.8 ng/mL (from nadir 0.6), meeting progression criteria.

Concurrently, Mr. Hutchinson has had significant difficulty with olaparib-related anemia. He developed grade 3 anemia (Hgb nadir 7.1 g/dL) at 3 months, requiring dose reduction to 200 mg BID and pRBC transfusion. He has since been transfusion-dependent with 8 units pRBC administered over the course of olaparib therapy (roughly every 4-6 weeks). Bone marrow biopsy obtained by hematology consultation showed erythroid hypoplasia consistent with PARP inhibitor toxicity; no marrow infiltration by tumor. He received IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) without meaningful improvement in transfusion independence. Given cumulative hematologic toxicity and now confirmed visceral + PSA progression, olaparib was discontinued at the last visit.

Repeat PSMA PET/CT obtained at olaparib discontinuation showed: 7 PSMA-avid bone lesions (T10, T12, L3, right 6th rib, left iliac wing, right posterior iliac crest, new right femoral head), PSMA-avid right iliac chain node (1.3 cm, SUVmax 6.1), and the segment VII hepatic lesion (SUVmax 8.4, now 1.8 cm). Notably, all lesions demonstrate strong PSMA avidity (SUVmax range 3.8-8.4). No brain lesions on skull survey.

His case was presented at GU Oncology Tumor Board. Multidisciplinary discussion included GU medical oncology, radiation oncology, nuclear medicine, palliative care, and pharmacy. The consensus recommendation, given his high tumor PSMA expression, post-ARSI/PARP-inhibitor status, and ECOG PS 2 with significant anemia, is to proceed with lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (177Lu-PSMA-617, Pluvicto) as next-line therapy. The team acknowledged that he has not yet received cabazitaxel-based chemotherapy; however, given his current performance status (ECOG 2), ongoing transfusion requirements, and strong patient preference to avoid IV cytotoxic chemotherapy, the group felt Pluvicto represented the most appropriate next step, noting that PSMA eligibility criteria are met. Palliative care remains actively co-managing symptoms. An extended goals-of-care conversation was held with Mr. Hutchinson and his wife.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue grade 2, limiting some daily activities. Reports 8-10 lb weight loss over the past 2 months. Decreased appetite, eating about 60% of usual intake. Denies fevers, night sweats.
- Musculoskeletal/Pain: Mid-back pain largely improved following T10 radiation (currently rates 2/10 at rest, 3/10 with activity). Mild discomfort at the right hip area, new over the past 3-4 weeks -- he attributes this to "stiffness" but does not describe it as sharp or incapacitating. No lower extremity weakness or numbness. No saddle anesthesia.
- GI: Mild right upper quadrant fullness/discomfort, intermittent. No jaundice. Nausea much improved since olaparib was stopped. Bowel function normal. No rectal bleeding.
- GU: Urinary frequency persistent (prior radiation effect), 2-3x nocturia. No gross hematuria. No obstructive symptoms beyond the above.
- Cardiovascular/Pulmonary: Dyspnea on exertion with activity above 1-2 flights of stairs, which he attributes to his anemia. Denies chest pain, palpitations, or orthopnea. No lower extremity edema.
- Neurological: No new neurological symptoms. No falls. Cognitive fogginess that had been attributed to enzalutamide has largely resolved.
- Endocrine: Hot flashes persistent on ADT, manageable.

MEDICATIONS:
- Leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (ADT -- continued)
- Denosumab (Xgeva) 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks (last dose 3 weeks ago)
- Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily (continued from prior abiraterone era; discussed tapering)
- Metformin 500 mg orally twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
- Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
- Ondansetron 4 mg orally every 8 hours as needed (nausea -- frequency decreasing since olaparib stopped)
- Omeprazole 20 mg orally daily
- Calcium carbonate 1250 mg orally twice daily + Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily (bone health with denosumab)
- Ferrous sulfate 325 mg orally twice daily (iron supplementation; patient notes poor tolerance)
- Acetaminophen 650 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (back pain)
- Oxycodone 5 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (new prescription from last visit for breakthrough pain; patient reports using 1-2 doses per day)

ALLERGIES: NKDA

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 128/76, HR 82, RR 16, Temp 98.2°F, O2 sat 96% on room air, Weight 171 lbs (down from 179 lbs 2 months ago)
General: Tired-appearing but pleasant gentleman in no acute distress, speaks in full sentences
Head/Neck: No icterus. No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
Chest/Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No accessory muscle use.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs. No lower extremity edema.
Abdomen: Soft. Mild tenderness to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant without guarding or rebound. No palpable hepatomegaly on examination, though patient reports subjective fullness. No ascites.
Musculoskeletal: Midline thoracic spine TTP at approximately T10 level, well-controlled per patient report. Mild tenderness to palpation over right greater trochanteric region. No bony deformity.
Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. CN II-XII grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in bilateral lower extremities. Sensation intact. Gait steady (with slight cautious quality, patient used cane today for the first time).
Skin: No rash. Pallor of mucous membranes consistent with anemia.
DRE: Deferred (prostate bed treated, no clinical indication today).

LABORATORY RESULTS (most recent, drawn today):
- PSA: 1.8 ng/mL (up from nadir 0.6 ng/mL)
- Testosterone: 8 ng/dL (castrate)
- CBC: WBC 4.1 K/uL, Hgb 8.1 g/dL (grade 2 anemia, last transfusion ~3 weeks ago), Hct 24.7%, Plt 187 K/uL, MCV 89 fL
- CMP: Na 138, K 4.1, Cr 1.2 mg/dL (eGFR 64 by CKD-EPI), ALT 38 U/L, AST 44 U/L (mildly elevated, up from 32/29 at last check -- hepatic metastasis effect), T.bili 0.9 mg/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL
- LDH: 312 U/L (elevated, baseline was 198)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 187 U/L (elevated, consistent with bone/hepatic involvement)

IMAGING (reviewed today):
Most recent PSMA PET/CT (68Ga-PSMA-11, obtained at olaparib discontinuation): As described above -- widespread PSMA-avid bone metastases (7 lesions), right iliac chain adenopathy, and a PSMA-avid hepatic lesion in segment VII. All sites with high SUVmax values. No PSMA-low/negative lesions identified. Compatible with eligibility criteria for lutetium-177 PSMA-617 per VISION trial criteria (adapted): at least one PSMA-positive lesion, no PSMA-negative visceral lesions that would indicate PSMA-negative clone predominance.

ASSESSMENT:
1. Metastatic castration-resistant prostate adenocarcinoma (mCRPC), BRCA2-mutated (germline and somatic biallelic loss), now with visceral (hepatic) metastases in addition to multifocal bone and nodal disease. Progressing on olaparib after prior enzalutamide, abiraterone, and definitive IMRT.
2. Treatment-related grade 2-3 anemia, transfusion-dependent, related to cumulative olaparib toxicity. Olaparib now discontinued. Hgb 8.1 g/dL today -- patient marginally compensated.
3. Right hip/groin discomfort -- given known right femoral head lesion on PSMA PET/CT, will need to closely monitor; orthopedic oncology consult considered if pain worsens or plain films suggest cortical involvement.
4. Mildly rising hepatic enzymes (AST/ALT) in setting of known hepatic metastasis -- to be monitored; not at a level requiring urgent intervention.
5. Weight loss and reduced appetite -- likely multifactorial (disease burden, prior olaparib, decreased physical activity). Palliative care and nutrition following.
6. Goals of care: Mr. Hutchinson has had a detailed discussion today regarding his prognosis and treatment goals. He understands his disease is currently not curable and that we are focused on disease control and preserving quality of life. He wishes to pursue active therapy at this time. He values maintaining functional independence and avoiding IV chemotherapy if possible. His wife is supportive and will assist with caregiving.

PLAN:
1. Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto): Proceed with scheduling per nuclear medicine/theranostics program. Standard protocol: 7.4 GBq (200 mCi) IV every 6 weeks x 6 cycles pending tolerability. Will obtain baseline labs including CBC, CMP, creatinine/eGFR (requirement for dosing), and urine analysis per protocol. Radiation safety counseling provided (standard isolation precautions for 48-72 hours post-infusion given household contacts). Pre-authorization to be submitted by care coordinator. Aim for first cycle in approximately 3-4 weeks once referral processed and pre-treatment workup complete. Continue ADT (leuprolide) throughout.
2. Anemia management: Hgb 8.1 g/dL today -- patient symptomatic on exertion. Will transfuse 2 units pRBC today given Hgb <8.5 and symptomatic dyspnea. Anticipate ongoing monitoring -- PARP inhibitor now off, some marrow recovery expected over 4-8 weeks but unclear timeline given degree of hypoplasia on biopsy. Will reassess with hematology.
3. Right hip pain: Plain films of right hip/pelvis ordered today to assess femoral head lesion for cortical integrity. Radiation oncology to be contacted if there is evidence of impending pathological fracture. Orthopedic oncology input if structural concern identified.
4. Hepatic metastasis: LFTs mildly elevated but stable enough to proceed with Pluvicto (no significant hepatic dysfunction). Repeat LFTs in 4 weeks at next visit or sooner if symptoms worsen.
5. Prednisone taper: Now that abiraterone has been off for some time, will taper prednisone over 4 weeks (5 mg BID -> 5 mg daily -> every other day -> off) to reduce immunosuppressive burden and cushingoid effects in setting of ongoing disease progression.
6. Pain control: Acetaminophen around-the-clock plus oxycodone as needed for breakthrough. Palliative care to reassess pain regimen at next visit. Patient counseled on early use of breakthrough medications.
7. Nutrition: Referral placed to oncology dietitian. Consider trial of low-dose megestrol or mirtazapine if anorexia and weight loss continue to worsen.
8. Denosumab: Continue Xgeva every 4 weeks for bone protection. Ensure dental clearance is up to date given ongoing use. Calcium/Vitamin D supplementation continued.
9. Follow-up: Return to clinic in 3-4 weeks for pre-treatment labs, repeat PSA, CBC, and finalization of Pluvicto scheduling. Patient instructed to call or present to ED immediately for any new neurological symptoms, severe pain, or signs of spinal cord compression.

PATIENT/FAMILY EDUCATION: Extensive discussion provided today regarding lutetium-177 PSMA-617 mechanism of action, expected response rates from clinical trial data, anticipated side effects (fatigue, cytopenias, nausea, xerostomia, radiation safety precautions). Patient and wife verbalized understanding. Written materials provided. Patient expressed cautious optimism about this treatment option.

Total visit time: 65 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in counseling and care coordination.

[Electronically signed by Attending Physician, GU Medical Oncology]
